title: Christ Knows, Changes, and Saves the Heart
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: The First Year of Jesus' Ministry

outline.title: Introduction: The Bible's Teaching on the Heart
subpoints: The biblical 'heart' is not the muscle in the chest but who a person is at his or her core: the seat of cognition, volition, and emotion
Proverbs 4:23: 'Keep your heart with all diligence, for out of it spring the issues of life'; Mark 7:21-23: from the heart proceeds all the evil we do
Jeremiah 17:9: 'The heart is deceitful above all things and desperately wicked' — God cares about what we do and say as a reflection of the heart
Three things Christ does to the heart, displayed in John 2:23-3:16: he knows it, he changes it, he saves it

outline.title: 1. Christ Knows Your Heart (John 2:23-3:2)
subpoints: Many people in Jerusalem saw Jesus' signs and 'believed in his name' (v.23) — but Jesus did not commit himself to them, because he knew all men
Their faith was not saving faith — perhaps belief in him as a miracle-worker or helpful teacher (cf. John 6: people followed him for bread, not for who he was)
Jesus knew their hearts supernaturally: Matthew 9:4 — Jesus perceived the Pharisees' thoughts; the Father revealed to him what was in man

Two applications: (1) Sobering — we cannot deceive God; if your faith is not genuine, he already knows; (2) Comforting — he knows every dark thought and hidden doubt, and still he loves and will never forsake those who are truly his

outline.title: 2. Christ Changes Your Heart (John 3:3-8)
subpoints: Nicodemus came with flattery — 'we know you are a teacher from God'; Jesus addressed what he actually needed: 'unless you are born from above, you cannot see the kingdom of God'
Nicodemus responded with ridicule (v.4) — not genuine confusion but sarcasm; the idea of new birth is foolishness to those whose hearts have not been changed (1 Cor. 2:14)
The failure is not in understanding but in receiving: Jesus says 'you do not receive our witness' — the issue is the will, not the intellect

The new birth is sovereignly initiated and completed by the Holy Spirit: (1) the analogy of birth — the baby has no say in being born; (2) the analogy of wind — it blows where it wills, beyond our control

Ezekiel 36:26-27: 'I will give you a new heart... I will take the heart of stone out of your flesh'; Ezekiel 37: God commands Ezekiel to preach to dry bones — life came through the proclaimed Word; that is why we preach
For the unbeliever under conviction: don't wait to see if the Spirit is working — just believe in Christ; don't waste the conviction God is bringing upon you

outline.title: 3. Christ Saves Your Heart (John 3:9-16)
subpoints: Nicodemus's sarcasm turned to genuine confusion: 'How can these things be?' — Jesus responds with appropriate rebuke: 'Are you the teacher of Israel and do not know these things?'
As Israel's leading scholar, Nicodemus should have known that the Old Testament taught God changes the heart — this plan of salvation is not new

The bronze serpent (Numbers 21): Israel rebelled, God sent serpents, judgment came, and in grace God commanded Moses to lift a bronze serpent on a pole — whoever looked in faith was healed; Jesus applies this to himself

The solution to the serpent's venom was not in the people — so the solution to the curse of sin is not in us; as the bronze serpent was lifted to heal, so Christ was lifted on the cross to remove the curse of the law for all who believe

John 3:16: God 'so loved' describes *how* he loved, not the intensity — the 'so' introduces the manner of God's love: 'in this way' — by giving his only Son; 'world' in John typically means all kinds of people, Jews and Gentiles
Nicodemus came to Jesus secretly by night; he is found openly at the foot of the cross, bringing spices to prepare the body — he came to saving faith in Christ who knows, changes, and saves the heart

summary: Continuing the series on the first year of Jesus' public ministry, Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on John 2:23-3:16 — Jesus' interaction with Nicodemus. Three things Christ does to the heart are traced through the passage: (1) Christ knows the heart — the people's faith in Jerusalem was not saving faith; Jesus knew this supernaturally, sobering us (we cannot deceive God) and comforting us (he knows our worst and still loves his own); (2) Christ changes the heart — Nicodemus's ridicule of new birth illustrates that unregenerate people cannot receive the gospel; new birth is sovereignly initiated by the Spirit (analogies of birth and wind); Ezekiel 36-37 promises and illustrates the heart of stone replaced by a heart of flesh through the proclaimed Word; (3) Christ saves the heart — the bronze serpent in Numbers 21 (judgment, grace, look in faith to be healed) prefigures the cross; John 3:16 describes how God loved — by giving his Son — so that all who believe receive eternal life. Nicodemus ended his story openly at the cross, transformed by the Christ who changed his heart.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Heart in Scripture

The Bible often speaks of the heart — and when it does, it rarely refers to the muscles in our chest that pump blood. The heart in Scripture is who a person is at his or her core: the seat of cognition, the seat of volition, the seat of emotion. The issues of life flow from the heart. Proverbs 4:23: *Keep your heart with all diligence, for out of it spring the issues of life.*

From the heart we think, do, and say. Our Lord says: *Out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks.* And the heart is corrupt. Jeremiah says: *The heart is deceitful above all things and desperately wicked.* Jesus says in Mark 7 that from within, out of the heart, proceed evil thoughts, adulteries, fornications, murders, thefts, covetousness, wickedness, deceit, lewdness, an evil eye, blasphemy, pride, foolishness — all these things come from within and defile a man.

God cares about what we do and say, but he cares about it as a reflection of what is in the heart. And this is displayed in Jesus' interaction with Nicodemus in our passage.

As we go through John 2:23-3:16, there are three things I want to show you: Christ knows your heart. Christ changes your heart. And Christ saves your heart.

## 1. Christ Knows Your Heart

As we progress through the Gospel of John and the first year of Jesus' public ministry, we see that there were many signs that Jesus performed which are not recorded — John tells us this explicitly in chapter 20. He includes the ones he does because they are for our profit.

Jesus had been doing many signs, and people who witnessed them developed a certain attitude toward him. John says in verse 23 that many *believed in his name* when they saw the signs. But we quickly find out that this was not saving faith. In verse 24, John says Jesus did not commit himself to them, *because he knew all men*.

They believed in him as a respectable teacher from God, or as a miracle-worker who could help them. We see this explicitly in John 6, where Jesus confronts the crowd: *You follow me not because you believe in who I am, but because I fed you bread.* These people were not genuinely united to Christ.

And Jesus knew this. There are two ways to understand his knowledge of the heart: first, as a wise man who read human nature clearly and understood the theology of the heart; second, as one to whom the Father supernaturally revealed what was in man. Matthew 9:4 shows us this: Jesus perceived the Pharisees' thoughts and replied to them. I believe the text intends the second: the Father supernaturally revealed to Jesus what was in the hearts of these people.

This supernatural knowledge of the heart enabled Jesus to see through Nicodemus's words and address what he actually needed.

Two applications from this:

*Sobering*: We cannot deceive God. We may deceive everyone else. We may live an entire life of deception. But we will never deceive God. The apostles addressed God as *Lord, who knows the hearts of all.* If your faith is not genuine — if you are going through the motions — repent now. He already knows. You are not deceiving him.

*Comforting*: Jesus knows every dark thought, every hidden doubt, every unfaithful moment in the remotest corners of your heart — and he still loves you. If you are clinging to Christ through genuine faith, he will never leave you or forsake you despite knowing you better than you know yourself. Our relationship with Christ is the safest relationship we have. We don't have to pretend. We don't have to impress.

But only those whose hearts have been changed can experience this safety — and that leads to the second point.

## 2. Christ Changes Your Heart

Nicodemus comes to Jesus in verse 2 with flattery: *Rabbi, we know that you are a teacher come from God, for no one can do these signs that you do unless God is with him.* Jesus bypassed the compliment entirely and went straight to what Nicodemus actually needed: *Most assuredly, I say to you, unless one is born from above, he cannot see the kingdom of God.*

Nicodemus responded with ridicule. Verse 4: *How can a man be born when he is old? Can he enter a second time into his mother's womb?* He is being sarcastic — mocking what Jesus just said. And why? Because the idea of a new birth is foolishness to those whose hearts have not been changed by the Spirit of God. Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 2:14: *The natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him.*

Notice: the failure is not in understanding. It is in *receiving*. In verse 11, Jesus says: *You do not receive our witness.* He does not say you do not understand it. He says you do not accept it. The will is the problem, not the intellect.

So Jesus explains what must happen: a new birth. Unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God. The birth of water is the natural, physical birth — the amniotic fluid. The birth of the Spirit is the supernatural birth by which God changes the heart. Verse 6: *That which is born of the flesh is flesh, and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit.*

And notice: this birth is sovereignly initiated and completed by the Holy Spirit — with no contribution from the person. Two analogies make this clear.

First, birth itself: the baby has no say in the process. You cannot decide to be born.

Second, wind: *The wind blows where it wishes, and you hear the sound of it, but cannot tell where it comes from and where it goes. So is everyone who is born of the Spirit.* The Spirit works independently of us. He works on us without our help.

This sovereign work of the Spirit is what God promised through Ezekiel: *I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you. I will take the heart of stone out of your flesh and give you a heart of flesh. I will put my Spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes.* That is the new covenant promise: God changes the heart.

And how does the Spirit ordinarily do this? Through preaching. Ezekiel 37: God brings his prophet to a valley of very dry bones — dead, lifeless. He commands Ezekiel to preach to the bones. And the breath came into them, and they lived — an exceedingly great army. That is why we proclaim the gospel to unbelievers. The Spirit changes hearts through the proclaimed Word.

For the unbeliever under conviction right now: don't wait to determine whether the Spirit is working. Just believe in the Lord Jesus Christ. Don't waste what God is bringing upon you. Turn from your sins and cling to Christ in faith.

## 3. Christ Saves Your Heart

Nicodemus's sarcasm has turned to genuine confusion. Verse 9: *How can these things be?* Jesus responds with appropriate rebuke: *Are you the teacher of Israel and do not know these things?*

As the leading scholar in Israel — likely the most respected teacher of his day — Nicodemus should have known that the Old Testament always taught this: God changes the heart. This plan of salvation is not new. And yet he couldn't receive it because his heart had not yet been changed.

Jesus presses the point: *If I have told you earthly things and you do not believe, how will you believe if I tell you heavenly things?* If the most basic element of the kingdom — that God changes hearts — is too much for you, how will you handle the greater glories? What we know of eternal life with God is only the beginning of what awaits.

Then Jesus gives Nicodemus the Old Testament key to understand what he is about to do. Numbers 21: Israel was in rebellion against God. God sent serpents among them. People were bitten and dying. In grace, God commanded Moses to craft a bronze serpent and lift it on a pole — and whoever looked at it in faith was healed.

Jesus says: *As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, even so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.*

As the bronze serpent was lifted to heal those under judgment, so Christ was lifted on the cross to remove the curse of the law from all who believe. The solution to the venom was not in the people looking — it was in the object of their faith. The solution for the curse of sin is not in us. It is in Christ, lifted up.

And why was it done this way? Verse 16: *For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have everlasting life.*

The word *so* here does not indicate the intensity of God's love — as if he loved us *so much*. It describes *the manner* in which he loved: *in this way* — by giving his Son. You could put a colon after it: 'God loved the world in this way: he gave his only Son.' The 'world' in John's Gospel typically refers to all kinds of people — Jews and Gentiles, not the total of every individual. God loved all kinds of people in this way: by giving his Son, so that absolutely everyone who believes that Jesus came to do for them what they could not do for themselves will not perish but have eternal life.

Nicodemus came to Jesus secretly, by night, slipping through the shadows so that no one would see him. He came hiding. But Nicodemus is found later — openly, publicly, at the foot of the cross, bringing spices to prepare the body of the Savior who died for him. He who came by night is found in the light. The Christ who knew his heart changed it and saved it.

Christ knows your heart — what a comfort to the believer, what a dreadful reality for those who pretend. Christ changes your heart — what great news, because we cannot do that ourselves. Christ saves your heart — what a glorious truth, that eternal life with God is offered to all who simply believe.

Father, thank you for the gift of the Lord Jesus Christ — who knows our hearts and changes them and saves them. Help us to rejoice in our salvation. For those not yet in Christ, change their hearts right now. We believe; help our unbelief. In Jesus' name, amen.
